GateDocument.TextWithNodes: "Queen Elizabeth II, Grace Kelly and Margot Robbie's go-to shoe label is reimaging the sneaker", "Roger Vivier's timeless designs have secured the brand a slew of famous clientele and now the brand looks to the future of footwear with its Viv' Sneaker.", "It's not every label that can command the attention of the world's most well-coiffed women, both past and present, but Roger Vivier boasts a notable and almost peerless bevy of supporters. Names like Aniston, Cruz and Dietrich - monikers so weighty they don't require first names - pad out the list of devotees. And even those whose last names have become superfluous over time: Amal, Margot, Jacqueline. It's not just Hollywood royalty either, with Vivier responsible for covering the impeccably polished feet of genuine royalty too, think: Grace Kelly and Queen Elizabeth II, who called on the footwear maestro to create the shoes for her 1953 Coronation. But how is it that a brand, now helmed by creative director Gherardo Felloni, born in the 1930s can consistently serve up beguiling pieces and rope in such notable supporters? Simple: timeless, innovative design. Vivier's sculptural shoes have captured the hearts of women from across the globe but his story starts in Paris on the rue Royale in 1937, where the designer first set up shop after finding success in made-to-measure footwear for Josephine Baker. A storied career would follow, with Vivier, who passed in 1998, invited to work with designers such as Christian Dior and Yves Saint Laurent. Credited with the invention of the stiletto, no small feat (pun intended), the Vivier brand is synonymous with pioneering design. Case in point: the 1959 debut of the 'Choc Heel', a striking concave heel, and the 1963 release of 'The Virgule', or comma, heel. But perhaps the brand's most iconic invention was the 'Belle Vivier' style, a black pump, topped with an oversized buckle, designed to complement Saint Laurent's Mondrian collection. The recognisable style is now firmly a part of the Vivier DNA and has been repeatedly reimagined by the brand - the iconic buckle finding its way across boots, ballerinas and mules over the years. It's the maison's heritage, in particular whispers of the 'Belle Vivier' style, that Felloni called on to inspire his latest more casual confections such as the Viv' Skate sneaker; which, inspired by '70s skateboarding culture, aims to capture the era's "youthful spirit" and "ideals of freedom and movement". Sneakers have become a key pillar of the modern women's wardrobe, no longer relegated to gym-only spheres, and moving beyond trends. Vivier's spring/summer 2021 collection, proves a thoughtful contribution to this shift, an offering of streamline silhouettes adorned with Maison's signature buckle, composed of 32 different crystals, all assembled by hand. Crafted in a slew of wearable shades - pink and yellow, as well as a white on white - and from an array of materials, including calfskin leather, velvet and grosgrain, these pieces prove the luxury sneaker is the epitome of contemporary chic. Need more proof? Drink up Felloni's designs, which now extend to all manner of luxury accessories, at David Jones and Westfield Sydney. Or simply refer to those aforementioned clients, those whose names, like Roger Vivier's, require no introduction."
